nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated coastal waters; commonly found attached to gorgonian corals, seagrass beds, mangrove roots, and floating Sargassum. Depth range 0–55 m, with smaller individuals generally in shallower water.

diet.notes: Generally accepts frozen mysid shrimp as a staple once acclimated; captive-bred specimens tend to adapt more readily than wild-caught individuals. Also takes vitamin-enriched adult brine shrimp, amphipods, and small crustaceans. Feeding two or more small meals per day is commonly advised. Slow, deliberate feeders that have been reported to lose out to faster-moving tankmates, so a species-only or pipefish-only tank tends to give the best results.

summary: Peaceful Western Atlantic seahorse with a slender body and long snout, found on gorgonians and seagrass. Generally advised to keep as mated pairs in a species-only or pipefish-only tank with hitching posts and multiple small daily feedings. Advanced care.
careNotes: Hippocampus reidi (Reidi Seahorse / Longsnout / Slender / Brazilian Seahorse) is generally considered one of the more rewarding seahorse species for experienced aquarists, with captive-bred specimens widely available and tending to be more tractable than wild-caught fish. A species-only or pipefish-only aquarium is strongly advised — faster-moving tankmates have been reported to outcompete seahorses for food, leading to malnutrition. Minimum tank around 50 US gallons (200 L) for a pair; a tank at least 45 cm (18 inches) tall is generally recommended to support natural courtship and swimming behavior. Water flow should be kept low to moderate; strong flow tends to stress seahorses and has been associated with increased bacterial infection risk. Provide ample hitching posts: gorgonians, branching corals, macroalgae, and artificial anchors. Temperature 22–26°C (72–79°F); staying in the lower-to-mid part of this range is commonly advised, as elevated temperatures appear to increase susceptibility to Vibrio infections. pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.021–1.025, dKH 8–12. Wild color forms include black, yellow, and orange morphs; coloration has been reported to shift in the aquarium depending on diet, lighting, and mood. IUCN status: Near Threatened; CITES Appendix II — captive-bred specimens are strongly preferred over wild-caught. Care-level disagreement noted: LiveAquaria rates care as 'Moderate'; FishBase/Wikipedia and specialist seahorse keepers commonly treat all seahorses as advanced due to feeding, water-quality, and disease-management demands; 'advanced' chosen here as the conservative aquarium-hobby consensus. Temperature cross-verification: LiveAquaria 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); FishBase wild environmental range 24–28°C (mean 26.7°C); a 22–26°C aquarium range used as a moderate average that keeps the upper bound below Vibrio-risk thresholds. Native-range note: LiveAquaria lists 'Western Caribbean' only, whereas FishBase and Wikipedia document a full Western Atlantic range from North Carolina (USA) through the Caribbean to southern Brazil; the broader documented range is used. LiveAquaria labels this species 'Venomous'; this appears to be a generic seahorse-handling warning rather than a true envenomating apparatus — seahorses do not have venomous spines in the lionfish/scorpionfish sense, so venomousSpines is recorded as false.
breedingNotes: Ovoviviparous; males brood eggs in a ventral pouch. Gestation has been reported at approximately two weeks; offspring are around 7 mm at birth and reach sexual maturity at roughly 8 cm. Pairs have been described as monogamous and home-ranging, with breeding seasons exceeding eight months in laboratory conditions. Has been successfully reared in captivity across multiple generations; fry require live zooplankton such as copepod nauplii and frequent feeding. Captive breeding is achievable but demanding.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Reidi Seahorse (Captive-Bred) product page. Primary source for care parameters, tank requirements, diet, color forms, and compatibility. Manifest URL pointed to the seahorses collection page; actual species page navigated directly.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (17.5 cm TL), taxonomy (Syngnathidae / Syngnathiformes), native range (Western Atlantic, USA to Brazil), depth (0–60 m), wild temperature range (24–28°C, mean 26.7°C), reproduction (ovoviviparous; ~2-week gestation; monogamous home-ranging pairs; maturity at 8 cm), and IUCN status (Near Threatened, 2016).

additional.name: Wikipedia (Longsnout seahorse)
additional.notes: Confirmed common names (Slender / Longsnout Seahorse), distribution (Bahamas, Belize, Bermuda, Brazil, Cuba, Florida/North Carolina USA, Venezuela, Caribbean nations), habitat (coral reefs, seagrass, gorgonians, mangroves, Sargassum), max size (~17.5 cm), gestation (~2 weeks), and conservation status (IUCN Near Threatened, CITES Appendix II). Used for native-range expansion beyond LiveAquaria's 'Western Caribbean'.

fish.breedingNotes: Male broods eggs in a ventral pouch; gestation about two weeks. Pairs tend to be monogamous and home-ranging. Has been bred across multiple captive generations; fry require live zooplankton (copepod nauplii) and frequent feeding.
